author: sproul
content: yeah agree

author: sproul
content: these compliance tests are hell for us lol, I'm curious to see another client run them

author: potuz
content:
What we currently do on our branch is to mark the proposer root for the first block that appears  that is timely. 

A simple way for us to address this issue would be to simply keep a list of proposer boosts instead of just the first one. We keep them ordered. We apply them or not only at the time of `get_head` anyway.

author: potuz
content: So for us effectively dealing with this would be really very simple

author: potuz
content: <@363800010518822915> are the tests that Michael mentions being skipped now in Prysm?

author: potuz
content: I thought we weren't skipping any new tests.

author: potuz
content: Or these are the new format ones from Misha?

author: sproul
content: they’re a similar format but they’re bundled separately from the main releases at the moment

author: potuz
content: ahh then we probably never ever ran them!

author: potuz
content: I have a PR to review for that...

author: potuz
content: at any rate, this particular issue does not seem to be too problematic to implement: just keep a list of block roots that you saw timely and decide on a call to get head whether or not you needed to apply them. I understand that this may fail a test that is set up explicitly exploiting the head changing during the call to on_block and the spec computing the boost to a different root. But I'm pretty sure the intended behavior is that one: that at the time you get to run forkchoice, you apply the boost or not depending to whether it was in the right shuffling.

author: potuz
content: Another way is to just precompute head at block insertion and just use that, those two work fine as far as I can see

author: sproul
content: I prefer this fix personally

author: potuz
content: both would be very simple to apply on PRysm so if this makes your life easier I'm happy to implement that

author: potuz
content: Just to be concrete, what I'd do is just check that the block I am inserting has the same dependent root in e-2 than the head block.

author: potuz
content: and apply boost to it under that condition

author: potuz
content: that's the simplest one for us to implement during block insertion as it's O(1)

author: sproul
content: yeah I think checking the dependent root is strictly better than checking the proposer index anyway, because it avoids an attack where the proposer is _coincidentally_ the same on some side chain, and can then bypass the protection to steal the boost

author: potuz
content: Loool that's sick but funnily enough you could in principle exploit it every single time you propose under no finality

author: potuz
content: at the cost of slashing yourself

author: potuz
content: Crap I implemented this and no unit test failed 😢 . Now I needed to add a regression for this case. Anyway it's a simple implementation if we accept the cached head as the source of truth.  https://github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/pull/16846

author: sproul
content: I've been in fork choice hell for the last week and we have so many bugs not previously covered by any test 🙃

author: potuz
content: lol

author: potuz
content: I don't want to implement these compliance tests now then 🙂

author: m.kalinin
content: I hope there is a space for a positive part in this feedback 😅  Thanks for running them and giving us the feedback -- this is very much appreciated. And finding discrepancies between the spec and implementations on various edge cases is one of the goals of those tests. As you may cover main paths with manually written tests but edge cases are often hard to identify

author: m.kalinin
content: Me too and I think we need to fix the spec
